title: Drilling into masonry
subtitle: Right bit, right mode, right depth
excerpt: How to drill into brick, block, and concrete correctly: choosing between a cordless combi drill and an SDS rotary hammer, selecting the correct masonry bit, setting the drill to hammer mode, drilling to the right depth, and clearing the hole for a wall plug.
sourceNotes: Standard UK masonry drilling practice. Bosch and Dewalt tool guidance for SDS and combi drill applications. Rawlplug and Fischer fixing manuals for hole size and wall plug compatibility.

recipeTools.notes: Sufficient for brick and soft block up to 8 mm diameter; the SDS is required for larger holes and concrete.
For marking the drilling depth on the bit with tape.
For checking the drill is horizontal.

content.text: Almost every domestic fixing into a masonry wall requires a drilled hole. Getting it right means the wall plug holds; getting it wrong means the plug spins, the screw strips, and the bracket falls off the wall. The two variables are the drill type and the bit size; both must match the wall material and the intended fixing.
Drill selection

A cordless combi drill in hammer mode is adequate for soft brick, medium-density block, and aerated block (Thermalite) up to about 8 mm bit diameter. For hard engineering brick, concrete, dense aggregate block, or holes larger than 8 mm, an SDS rotary hammer drill is required. The SDS action breaks the material with a rapid forward strike while the bit rotates; a combi drill cannot replicate this in hard masonry.
Bit selection

Masonry bits have a tungsten carbide tip and a spiral flute for clearing the dust. The bit diameter must match the wall plug. Standard UK fixing sizes: 5 mm bit for a 5 mm / No. 8 plug; 6 mm bit for a 6 mm / No. 10 plug; 8 mm for a 8 mm heavy-duty plug. Using a smaller bit than the plug leaves the hole too tight; the plug will not expand. Using a larger bit leaves the hole too loose; the plug will spin.
Preparation
Step 1, check for services.

Before drilling any wall, check for concealed pipes and cables with a detector. Electric cables run vertically from sockets and switches and horizontally at ceiling and floor level; pipes run in similar patterns. A drill through a cable or pipe is a serious incident. If in doubt, do not drill.
Step 2, mark the hole.

Mark the hole position with a centre-punch mark in the plaster to stop the bit skidding on the smooth surface. Mark the required drill depth on the bit with masking tape: depth equals the wall plug length plus 5 mm to allow the dust to clear.
Drilling
Step 3, start the hole at low speed.

Set the drill to hammer mode and start at low speed until the bit is seated in the wall surface. Then increase to full speed. Press steadily without forcing; the hammer action breaks the material without continuous pressure. Withdraw the bit every 30 seconds to clear the dust from the flutes.
Step 4, drill to depth.

Drill until the masking tape depth marker is level with the wall surface. If you hit a hard spot (a flint, a dense aggregate stone), do not force through it; withdraw, reposition slightly, and re-approach. Hard engineering brick simply requires more time at full speed.
Step 5, clear the hole.

Blow the dust from the hole with a puff of breath or a can of compressed air. A hole full of dust prevents the plug from expanding to its full diameter. Insert the wall plug; it should push in by hand until its shoulder is flush with the plaster face.
Common problems
Bit not making progress despite the drill running.
The bit has hit a hard aggregate stone or is dull. Withdraw, check the bit tip is undamaged, and try a fresh start point 10 mm to one side of the original hole.
Wall plug spinning when the screw is driven.
The hole is too large. Fill it with a slightly oversize plug, or use an expanding bolt-type anchor designed for oversized holes.
Drilling into the mortar joint rather than the brick.
Mortar is softer than brick and plugs grip poorly in it. Reposition the fixing so the hole centre falls on solid brick or block, not on a mortar joint.
